refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the customer-defined evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is capable of performing its intended mission to total operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it explicitly defines the measurement basis (availability ratio), the target threshold (≥0.97), the evaluation period scope, and acknowledges that availability is a system-level emergent property dependent on vehicle reliability, teleoperation robustness, safety service responsiveness, and maintenance effectiveness. High safety criticality requires that safety-driven shutdowns are counted as planned downtime, not failures.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry logging of system state transitions (operational, maintenance, safety hold, fault) across all modules; post-evaluation statistical analysis of uptime vs. total time; traceability to root causes of downtime events; customer sign-off on measurement methodology and evaluation period.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall maintain mechanical and electrical reliability such that unplanned downtime due to vehicle faults does not exceed 3% of operational time.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall maintain network link availability and command/response latency such that tele-operator-induced downtime does not exceed 1% of operational time.

FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall detect and respond to hazardous conditions within safety-critical time windows, with no safety-driven shutdowns exceeding 2% of operational time due to false positives or slow response.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE Solution shall provide predictive maintenance scheduling and fault diagnostics to enable planned maintenance windows that do not exceed 2% of operational time.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant subsystems (dual Vehicle, dual Teleoperation links, dual Safety Services) with automatic failover to achieve 0.97 availability through hardware redundancy.

DP-B: Implement continuous health monitoring, predictive maintenance scheduling, and rapid fault isolation/recovery across all subsystems to minimize unplanned downtime and optimize planned maintenance windows.
DP-C: Implement a hierarchical availability management architecture with real-time state tracking, automated failover logic, and closed-loop feedback from telemetry to maintenance planning.
DP-D: Implement modular design with standardized interfaces and rapid component swap capability, combined with spare parts inventory management and on-site maintenance capability.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement a hierarchical availability management architecture with real-time state tracking, automated failover logic, and closed-loop feedback from telemetry to maintenance planning. This DP balances cost, complexity, and effectiveness by leveraging continuous monitoring (DP-B concept) and modular design (DP-D concept) without requiring full hardware redundancy (DP-A), while providing the system-level orchestration needed to meet the 0.97 target across heterogeneous subsystems.

potential_sources: DP-C requires tight coupling between Safety Services (for hazard detection triggers) and Vehicle/Teleoperation (for graceful shutdown/failover); this is acceptable because safety criticality demands synchronous response.

DP-C requires bidirectional telemetry flow from Vehicle and Teleoperation to a central availability manager; this introduces a single point of observation but not a single point of failure if the manager is stateless and distributed.
DP-C assumes predictive maintenance algorithms can be trained on historical fault data; coupling to maintenance planning process is organizational, not technical.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP avoids the tight coupling of full redundancy (DP-A) while maintaining the observability and responsiveness needed for a safety-critical system. Decoupling risk is low if interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) are well-defined.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Telemetry data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems is available in real-time with sufficient fidelity to detect incipient faults before they cause downtime.
ASSUMPTION: Safety Services can provide early warning signals (SafetyAlert) that allow graceful shutdown rather than abrupt failure.
RISK: If telemetry is delayed or incomplete, the availability manager cannot respond in time; mitigation: define telemetry SLAs (latency, completeness) as part of DP realization.
RISK: Predictive maintenance algorithms may have high false-positive rates, leading to unnecessary planned downtime; mitigation: tune algorithms iteratively based on field data.

RISK: Organizational processes (maintenance scheduling, spare parts logistics) may not keep pace with technical availability targets; mitigation: allocate availability budget to organizational factors in sub-FR decomposition.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) the availability manager's architecture (centralized vs. distributed), (2) the telemetry SLAs (latency, completeness, accuracy), (3) the failover decision logic (thresholds, timing), and (4) the feedback loop from maintenance outcomes to predictive models. This will reduce ambiguity when assigning to implementation modules.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary that orchestrates availability management across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. It owns the hierarchical availability management architecture (DP-C), including real-time state tracking, automated failover logic, and closed-loop feedback to maintenance planning. The module integrates telemetry from all subsystems, synthesizes safety alerts, and coordinates graceful degradation and recovery.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Continuous health and state data from Vehicle (mechanical, electrical, fuel, position, load) and Teleoperation (link quality, latency, command queue depth). Used by availability manager to detect faults and trigger predictive maintenance.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Real-time hazard notifications from Safety Services subsystem (e.g., proximity warnings, stability alerts, operator override signals). Triggers immediate failover or graceful shutdown logic.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Mission commands and operational directives from Site Manager or Tele-operator. Availability manager uses task context to optimize maintenance scheduling and failover decisions (e.g., defer non-critical maintenance during high-priority tasks).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication backbone for all inter-module messaging (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, and internal availability manager commands to Vehicle and Teleoperation). Availability manager monitors link health and triggers failover if primary link degrades.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of the availability management architecture. It has direct visibility to all subsystems via Telemetry and SafetyAlert, and it orchestrates failover and maintenance scheduling. Minimal diffusion: the core logic (state tracking, failover decision, feedback loop) is contained within this module. Minor diffusion: some availability-related logic may reside in Vehicle (self-diagnostics) and Safety Services (hazard detection), but these are subsystem-level concerns, not system-level orchestration.

Vehicle is responsible for its own mechanical and electrical reliability (sub-FR-004.1), but it should not own the system-level availability manager. If availability logic is distributed into Vehicle, it becomes difficult to coordinate failover across subsystems and to maintain a unified view of system state. Vehicle should emit Telemetry and respond to availability manager commands, but not orchestrate system-level decisions.

Teleoperation is responsible for maintaining link availability and command/response latency (sub-FR-004.2), but it should not own the system-level availability manager. Similar to Vehicle, distributing availability logic into Teleoperation would fragment the orchestration layer. Teleoperation should emit Telemetry (link quality, latency) and respond to failover commands, but not make system-level decisions.

Safety Services is responsible for hazard detection and response (sub-FR-004.3), which is a critical input to availability management. However, Safety Services should not own the system-level availability manager because its primary concern is safety, not availability optimization. Safety Services should emit SafetyAlert and provide early warning signals, but the availability manager (in NGHE Solution) should decide how to respond (e.g., graceful shutdown vs. failover).

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the optimal owner of the hierarchical availability management architecture because: (1) it has system-level visibility to all subsystems via Telemetry and SafetyAlert, (2) it can make coordinated failover and maintenance scheduling decisions without fragmenting logic across subsystems, (3) it can implement the closed-loop feedback mechanism (telemetry → predictive models → maintenance planning) at the system level, and (4) it avoids diffusion of availability logic into Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, which should focus on their respective domain concerns (reliability, link quality, safety).
containment_risks: RISK: NGHE Solution becomes a large, complex module if it owns not only availability management but also other system-level concerns (e.g., mission planning, resource allocation). Mitigation: clearly scope NGHE Solution to availability management and system integration; delegate domain-specific logic to subsystems.

RISK: Availability manager may become a bottleneck if it is centralized and synchronous. Mitigation: design the availability manager as a distributed, event-driven system with asynchronous telemetry processing and failover logic.

RISK: Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces may not provide sufficient fidelity for the availability manager to make timely decisions. Mitigation: define telemetry SLAs and safety alert response times as part of DP realization; validate against sub-FR targets (3%, 1%, 2% downtime budgets).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine the NGHE Solution module boundary to explicitly separate: (1) the availability manager (core logic for state tracking, failover, maintenance scheduling), (2) the telemetry aggregator (collects and normalizes data from Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), and (3) the system integration layer (coordinates inter-module communication via Network Link). This will improve clarity and enable independent testing of each component.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR (CR-NGHE-004) clearly specifies the availability target (≥0.97), measurement basis (uptime ratio), and evaluation period. The selected DP (hierarchical availability management with real-time state tracking, automated failover, and closed-loop feedback) directly addresses the FR by providing the mechanisms to detect faults, respond quickly, and optimize maintenance. The DP is not over-constrained (it allows multiple implementation approaches) and is not under-constrained (it specifies the key architectural decisions: centralized state tracking, automated failover, predictive maintenance feedback). Coupling is acceptable given the safety-critical nature of the system.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK: If availability logic is scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules, the system-level orchestration becomes fragmented and difficult to verify. Mitigation: NGHE Solution module owns the availability manager; subsystems emit telemetry and respond to commands.

DIFFUSION RISK: If the availability manager is implemented as a monolithic component within NGHE Solution, it may become difficult to test and maintain. Mitigation: decompose the availability manager into telemetry aggregator, state tracker, failover logic, and maintenance scheduler; assign clear ownership and interfaces.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The NGHE Solution module has a containment score of 0.95 for the availability management DP, indicating strong alignment. The module has direct access to all required inputs (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking) and can orchestrate outputs (failover commands, maintenance scheduling) without requiring cross-module dependencies. The 0.05 diffusion score reflects minor logic that may reside in subsystems (e.g., Vehicle self-diagnostics), which is acceptable because it is subsystem-level, not system-level.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is verifiable through continuous telemetry logging and post-evaluation statistical analysis. The NGHE Solution module can emit availability metrics (uptime, downtime by category, root causes) that directly map to the FR. Sub-FRs (004.1, 004.2, 004.3, 004.4) provide intermediate verification points that can be traced to subsystem performance. Verification is feasible if telemetry SLAs are defined and subsystems provide the required data.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of CR-NGHE-004 to NGHE Solution is clear: the module owns the system-level availability management architecture. The allocation of sub-FRs to subsystems is also clear: Vehicle owns 004.1 (mechanical/electrical reliability), Teleoperation owns 004.2 (link availability), Safety Services owns 004.3 (hazard detection/response), and NGHE Solution owns 004.4 (predictive maintenance and diagnostics). Each sub-FR has a clear owner and measurable target (3%, 1%, 2%, 2% downtime budgets).

recommended_revisions: Refine the DP to specify telemetry SLAs (latency, completeness, accuracy) and failover decision thresholds. This will reduce ambiguity when implementing the availability manager.

Refine the NGHE Solution module boundary to separate the availability manager into sub-components (telemetry aggregator, state tracker, failover logic, maintenance scheduler) with clear interfaces. This will improve testability and maintainability.

Define the evaluation period and measurement methodology in the refined FR. This will ensure that the customer and the development team have a shared understanding of how availability will be measured and verified.

Allocate availability budget to organizational factors (maintenance scheduling, spare parts logistics, operator training) in addition to technical factors. This will ensure that the 0.97 target is achievable in practice.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR has identified four sub-FRs (004.1, 004.2, 004.3, 004.4) that allocate the availability target to subsystems and system-level functions. Each sub-FR is more specific and measurable than the parent FR, and each has a clear owner (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, or NGHE Solution). The selected DP (hierarchical availability management) is sound and well-aligned with the NGHE Solution module, but it requires further refinement at the next level to specify the detailed architecture, interfaces, and algorithms. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will enable more detailed design and verification planning.
decision.justification_detail: The FR-DP-IM alignment is strong at this level, but the DP is still at a conceptual level. The next decomposition level should address: (1) the detailed architecture of the availability manager (centralized vs. distributed, synchronous vs. asynchronous), (2) the telemetry aggregation and normalization logic, (3) the state tracking and failover decision algorithms, (4) the predictive maintenance scheduling logic, and (5) the interfaces between the availability manager and subsystems. This will enable more detailed design, testing, and verification.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.4.1: The NGHE Solution shall aggregate and normalize telemetry data from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems into a unified system state representation within 100 ms of data generation.

FR-NGHE-004.4.2: The NGHE Solution shall detect incipient faults and anomalies in subsystem health data using predictive models and alert the maintenance planning system with sufficient lead time to schedule preventive maintenance before failure occurs.

FR-NGHE-004.4.3: The NGHE Solution shall execute automated failover logic to gracefully degrade or recover system functionality in response to subsystem faults or safety alerts, with failover decision latency ≤ 500 ms.

FR-NGHE-004.4.4: The NGHE Solution shall provide closed-loop feedback from maintenance outcomes (actual vs. predicted failures, maintenance duration, spare parts usage) to predictive models to continuously improve availability forecasting.

assumptions: Telemetry data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems is available in real-time with sufficient fidelity to detect incipient faults.
Safety Services can provide early warning signals (SafetyAlert) that allow graceful shutdown rather than abrupt failure.
Predictive maintenance algorithms can be trained on historical fault data and will improve over time with field feedback.
Organizational processes (maintenance scheduling, spare parts logistics) can be optimized to support the 0.97 availability target.
The customer-defined evaluation period is at least 30 days to provide statistically significant availability data.
Safety-driven shutdowns are counted as planned downtime, not failures, in the availability calculation.
open_risks: Telemetry latency or incompleteness may prevent the availability manager from detecting faults in time to prevent downtime.
Predictive maintenance algorithms may have high false-positive rates, leading to unnecessary planned downtime.
Failover logic may introduce new failure modes (e.g., cascading failures, deadlocks) that reduce availability.
Organizational processes may not keep pace with technical availability targets, leading to missed maintenance windows or spare parts shortages.
The 0.97 availability target may be unachievable without full hardware redundancy, which was rejected as too costly.
Safety Services may not provide sufficient early warning time for graceful shutdown, forcing abrupt failover.
evidence_needed: Telemetry SLAs (latency, completeness, accuracy) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems.
Safety alert response time requirements from Safety Services subsystem.
Historical fault data from similar equipment to train predictive maintenance models.
Organizational process documentation (maintenance scheduling, spare parts logistics, operator training).
Customer-defined evaluation period and measurement methodology for availability.
Failover decision thresholds and latency requirements for automated failover logic.
Availability budget allocation across subsystems (3%, 1%, 2%, 2% downtime targets).
